data.title: Futu Holdings upgraded to Buy/High Risk at Citi as prospects improve for Q4
data.description:
Citi upgraded Futu Holdings (FUTU) to Buy/High Risk from Neutral/High Risk after the online brokerage's stock slid 8% since it posted Q3 results. Meanwhile, Citi expects robust client asset inflow and trading volume will continue to support Futu's brokerage commission and interest income.

In addition, the company's US$800M stock buyback programs should also support the stock.

Futu (FUTU) stock rose 3.0% in Friday morning trading.

"After the recent share price correction (–8% since 3Q25 results on 18 November and –23% MTD), Futu is trading at 13.6x FY26E PE with FY26E ROE of 26.9% and shareholder capital return of 25%, which we see as an attractive risk-reward profile," analyst Judy Zhang wrote in a note to clients.

Management expects stable Q/Q client asset balance and sequential growth in client asset inflow to offset the mark-to-market loss quarter-to-date, the analyst noted. Trading volume, too, is expected to record sequential growth.

Citi's Buy rating on Futu (FUTU) aligns with the SA Quant rating and average Wall Street rating, both at Strong Buy, and contrasts with the average SA Analyst rating of Hold.

data.title: UGI outlines 5% to 7% EPS CAGR target through 2029 while strengthening AmeriGas operations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UGI Corporation (UGI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Flexon, CEO, announced that "UGI delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $3.32 through strong execution across multiple fronts, surpassing our revised guidance range of $3 to $3.15." Flexon highlighted strengthened balance sheet, $530 million of free cash flow generation (including asset sales), and continued dividend payments. He referenced upgrades in pipeline infrastructure, new LNG and renewable natural gas facilities, and portfolio optimization at UGI International, which generated $150 million from LPG divestitures, with the Austrian business divestiture pending.

  • Flexon stated, "We are raising our long-term EPS growth expectations with a new EPS compound annual growth rate target of 5% to 7%." He attributed this to intrinsic opportunities and confidence in strategic execution.

  • Michael Sharp, President of AmeriGas Propane, detailed five strategic pillars: safety, customer relationships, process efficiency, employee engagement, and financial discipline. Sharp explained, "After performing a customer profitability assessment, we decided to exit the wholesale business that represented roughly 11% of our total volumes, but was largely a breakeven business." He noted investments in call center reshoring, AI adoption, billing simplification, and routing improvements with initial pilots demonstrating "approximately 10% savings in fuel costs."

  • Sean O’Brien, CFO, reported, "UGI delivered impressive results in fiscal 2025 with adjusted diluted EPS of $3.32, $0.26 higher than the prior year." O’Brien broke down segment performance, noting AmeriGas EBIT growth of $24 million (17%) and record EBIT of $403 million at regulated utilities. Cash generation exceeded $500 million in free cash flow, with $320 million returned to shareholders via dividends. O’Brien stated, "Yesterday, we announced our fiscal 2026 guidance range for adjusted diluted EPS of $2.85 to $3.15," assuming normal weather and current tax environment.

Outlook

  • O’Brien communicated fiscal 2026 guidance for adjusted diluted EPS of $2.85 to $3.15. He projected 5% to 7% reportable segment EBIT growth year-over-year and a long-term EPS CAGR of 5% to 7% through 2029, supported by $4.5 billion to $4.9 billion in capital investment. Utilities expect higher gas base rates and continued customer growth. Midstream & Marketing is projected for continued earnings growth underpinned by fee-based margins. AmeriGas expects year-over-year growth in retail volume and EBIT due to ongoing transformation. UGI International is expected to remain in line with current year results.

Financial Results

  • UGI reported adjusted diluted EPS of $3.32 for fiscal 2025. AmeriGas achieved $0.27 in EPS and $166 million in EBIT, a $24 million increase over the prior year. The regulated utilities segment recorded record EBIT of $403 million, with a $39 million increase in total margin stemming from a 10% rise in core market volumes. The utility segment added over 11,500 customers in the year. Midstream & Marketing EBIT was $293 million, down $20 million, while UGI International reported $314 million of EBIT, $9 million below the prior year, with a 4% LPG volume decline. Operating and administrative expenses decreased in both AmeriGas and UGI International due to cost control measures and business exits.

Q&A

  • Gabriel Moreen, Mizuho Securities: Asked about guidance assumptions for midstream and LPG businesses and expectations for future growth. Flexon responded, "We expect to have growth in all of the business lines overall. So we'll see low double-digit growth over that planning period."

  • Moreen followed up on natural gas activity and data center demand in Pennsylvania. Flexon confirmed, "The amount of NDAs that we have with counterparties is north of 50...significant discussions underway and in various stages."

  • Moreen referenced media reports on potentially selling the electric utility. Flexon replied, "We will continuously look at our portfolio for those opportunities...portfolio optimization continues to be one of the things that we will always consider."

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: Queried on AmeriGas leverage targets and growth drivers. O’Brien detailed, "This year, we went from a leverage ratio of about 6...to 4.9, which we're incredibly proud of. That happened in 2 ways. Mike and the team grew EBIT $24 million, 17%. Obviously, that has a positive impact. And we delevered another $200 million."

  • Dumoulin-Smith asked about normalization post-tax credits. O’Brien explained, "We have no expectations going forward on any ITCs, although we do have...about $0.09 of PTCs in the ongoing forecast so much lower level."

  • Dumoulin-Smith asked about CapEx allocation. O’Brien answered, "The utility CapEx...pretty consistent, maybe even slightly up...You do see a little more midstream capital coming into the equation, and...our commitment to the dividend in the out years as well."

  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg Thalmann: Asked about 45Z credits. Flexon responded, "It will be '26 will be the first time." On calculation approach, Flexon said, "Not sure about that one. We'll need to get back to you on that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on segment growth, capital allocation, and the impact of tax credits, with a tone that was neutral to slightly skeptical, especially regarding AmeriGas targets and the sustainability of tax-related benefits.

  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, frequently referencing strong performance, operational improvements, and future growth. Flexon expressed confidence: "We are raising our long-term EPS growth expectations." O’Brien noted, "We feel very confident there."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared even more assertive about operational progress and long-term growth, while analysts maintained a consistent level of scrutiny, especially regarding normalization and future sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a formal increase in the long-term EPS growth target (5% to 7% CAGR) and a raised focus on AmeriGas operational transformation and profitability.

  • The fiscal 2026 guidance was presented, whereas last quarter, management only discussed being at the top end of the 2025 guidance. Analysts continued to focus on AmeriGas performance and portfolio optimization, but pressed further on capital allocation and leverage targets this quarter.

  • Key metrics such as EPS, EBIT growth, and leverage improvement were emphasized this quarter, with AmeriGas highlighted as a turnaround story, compared to last quarter’s broader discussion of seasonal performance and strategic divestitures.

  • Management’s tone shifted from emphasizing resilience and foundational improvements to highlighting specific operational wins and quantifiable growth targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management referenced anticipated higher interest expense and tax rate normalization in fiscal 2026 due to the absence of $0.40 in investment tax credits received in fiscal 2025.

  • Analysts raised concerns about AmeriGas leverage reduction and the sustainability of recent tax credit benefits.

  • Management acknowledged portfolio optimization as an ongoing consideration and indicated continued review of asset holdings for value creation.

Final Takeaway

UGI management reported a record year, driven by operational improvements and strategic portfolio actions, supporting a new 5% to 7% EPS CAGR target through 2029. AmeriGas demonstrated significant EBIT growth and efficiency gains, and the company expects continued growth across all segments, underpinned by disciplined capital deployment and ongoing portfolio optimization. The fiscal 2026 outlook assumes normalized tax and weather environments, with management expressing confidence in sustaining growth and strengthening the balance sheet for long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oct. CPI will not be published, Nov. CPI slated for Dec. 18, BLS says
data.description:
The Bureau of Labor Statistics said Friday it will not publish the October Consumer Price Index report because it could not retroactively collect reference period survey data due to a lapse in appropriations.

The Labor Department's BLS noted that it's able to retroactively acquire most of the non-survey data for October. "Where possible, BLS will publish October 2025 values for these series with the release of November 2025 data," it said.

On the November retail inflation report, the BLS will publish it on Dec. 18, 2025, though one-month percent changes will not be included since the October data are missing.

data.title: Wall Street edges higher after the prior day’s volatile downturn
data.description:
Wall Street edged higher on Friday, a day after the Nasdaq Composite saw a dramatic session due to a tech-led sell-off.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last up by 0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had inched up by 0.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) advanced by 0.7%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, eight of the 11 S&P segments are tracking higher in the green and are led by Communication Services. At the other end of the spectrum, Info Tech has been the worst performer on the session so far. 

Treasury yields continue to tick lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 2 basis points to 4.06% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 2 basis points to 3.51%. 

Market participants are also still digesting a turbulent session on Thursday that sent the Nasdaq Composite on a dramatic round trip—from up more than 2% in the morning to down over 2% at the close.

“While we expect volatility in 2026, we do expect a positive return with the S&P 500 reaching 7500 by the end of next year,” Mary Ann Bartels, chief investment strategist at Sanctuary Wealth said. 

“As we enter 2026, the equity market has had great performance since the lows of the bear market in 2022. We expect 2026 to be the reset year for the leadership of the market in the big Growth Technology stocks. We believe this reset will position stocks to continue their leadership into the end of the decade. Despite this anticipated correction in the large cap area of the market, we don’t see leadership emerging from small caps,” Mary Ann Bartels added. 

On the economic front, there are a few Federal Reserve speakers lined up, including Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan and Boston Fed President Susan Collins. 

The S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI rose slightly to 54.8 in November from 54.6 in the prior month. At the same time, Services PMI came in at 55 and manufacturing PMI came in at 51.9.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Intuit Inc (INTU) are higher by 5%, while shares of Oracle (ORCL) have slid lower by 6.4%.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: GILD, UBER, FROG, JACK
data.description:
Recent analyst actions show a stark divide in market sentiment, with notable downgrades for Jack in the Box (JACK) reflecting struggles with inflation and competition, while Gilead Sciences (GILD) received a positive upgrade based on its HIV division strength. Uber Technologies (UBER) also gained an upgrade amid promising autonomous vehicle partnerships with Nvidia, positioning it well for future growth despite near-term unprofitability. Meanwhile, JFrog (FROG) faced a downgrade to Sell despite strong performance, as its valuation has stretched far beyond industry peers in the software development space.

Upgrades

  • Gilead Sciences (GILD): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Edmund Ingham. The upgrade reflects Gilead’s continued outperformance driven by its dominant HIV division and strategic M&A activity, with analysts noting no major patent expirations until 2036.

    “Since 2018, the HIV division has doubled in size, from $9.1bn per annum, to $19.6bn in 2024… Ultimately, I feel as though in the past Gilead itself, and Wall Street analysts have arguably focused too much on what Gilead is not - an oncology / immunology powerhouse - and not enough on what it is - an HIV powerhouse with no major patent expiries due until 2036.”

  • Uber Technologies (UBER): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Vinay Utham, CFA. The analyst cites UBER’s strategic autonomous vehicle partnerships, particularly with Nvidia, as key catalysts positioning the company for long-term AV leadership despite near-term profitability challenges.

    “The partnership with NVIDIA, to me, has more to do with rideshare data, which is vital if the AV revolution has to take off. Given that UBER has one of the biggest global presences relative to its peers, the amount of data that UBER will have, through the NVIDIA partnership, will be a goldmine for any AV player… The partnership with NVIDIA may be at its early stages and is unlikely to contribute to the company’s bottom-line in the near-term future. However, to me, this partnership is what would enable UBER to remain one step ahead of its rivals.”

Downgrades

  • Jack in the Box (JACK): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Daniel Javier. The company remains vulnerable to inflationary headwinds with squeezed sales and margins, while facing fierce competition that continues to hurt demand.

    “Jack in the Box, Inc. remains hammered and does not indicate recovery amid stubborn inflation and predatory competition. The sale of Del Taco may open opportunities to improve its margins and liquidity, but it will be hard for it to regain its previous position. Today, valuation makes sense and has already priced in risks and disposal opportunities but remains almost fully valued.”

  • JFrog (FROG): Downgrade to Sell by Gary Alexander. Despite excellent execution and improved net retention rates, JFrog’s valuation has become stretched at 12.7x FY25 revenue and 58x FY25 FCF, far exceeding peers in the enterprise software space.

    “Make no mistake, I think JFrog is producing excellent results. At the same time, however, I don’t see an opportunity for the company to see a continued appreciation in valuation multiples, which is what drove the majority of its gains this year as the company expanded from a mid-single-digit revenue multiple to a mid-teens multiple that now towers over peers. To me, this overvaluation carries tremendous risk of correction as we enter into a risk-off attitude in the stock market.”

data.title: LLY joins $1T club but doesn't feature in SA's top 10 large-cap healthcare stocks
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY) officially joined the elite group of U.S. companies valued at more than $1T on Friday, becoming the tenth American stock to currently cross the landmark threshold.

The move came as shares advanced 1%, driven by persistent investor enthusiasm for the pharmaceutical giant’s fast-growing GLP-1 drug franchise, which continues to reshape expectations across the broader health care sector. 

Despite achieving the trillion-dollar milestone, Lilly (LLY) does not currently rank among Seeking Alpha’s top ten healthcare stocks based on its Quant Ratings framework. 

According to the SA’s proprietary scoring system, LLY holds a Quant Rating of 3.47, placing it outside the upper tier. Below is a breakdown of the highest-rated healthcare names as measured by Seeking Alpha’s Quant Grades (note that these are only companies with a market cap of $10B and above): 

No. 1: Guardant Health (GH), Quant Rating of 4.95.

No. 2: Incyte Corporation (INCY), Quant Rating of 4.93.

No. 3: Pfizer (PFE), Quant Rating of 4.91.

No. 4: Merck & Co. (MRK), Quant Rating of 4.87.

No. 5: Roivant Sciences Ltd. (ROIV), Quant Rating of 4.82.

No. 6: Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), Quant Rating of 4.70.

No. 7: Astellas Pharma Inc. (OTCPK:ALPMY), Quant Rating of 4.69.

No. 8: Illumina (ILMN), Quant Rating of 4.62.

No. 9: Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX), Quant Rating of 4.58.

No. 10: Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL), Quant Rating of 4.58.

For more on the healthcare space, investors can look to gain diversified exposure to the space through exchange-traded funds. 

Health Care ETFs: (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), (FXH), (IBB), (XBI), (FBT), (BBH), (PBE), (IDNA), (ARKG), (GNOM), (BIB), and (HELX).

data.title: Doximity upgraded at Raymond James on improving long-term growth visibility
data.description:
• Raymond James upgraded Doximity (DOCS) to strong buy from outperform, saying that the digital platform for doctors with a "current risk/reward at 25x [free cash flow] looking too compelling to ignore."
  • The firm, however, lowered its target price to $65 from $75 (40% upside based on Nov. 20 close).
  • Analyst Brian Peterson notes that while some investors have concerns about seasonality and guidance philosophy, the company has also been ramping up workflow adoption, multi-product expansion, and client portal benefits.
  • Peterson said that Doximity has seen annual growth rates from 2023 to 2025 of 15%-18%, with quarterly growth rates in that period experiencing peaks in the mid-20% range and troughing in the mid-single digit range. This, he added, is a sign that "seasonal shifts are not indicative of longer-term growth rates."
  • "...we're comfortable underwriting a double-digit growth trajectory for the foreseeable future," Peterson wrote.

data.title: Strategy's financing model tested as preferreds slide with bitcoin - report
data.description:
Strategy's (MSTR) preferred shares fell alongside bitcoin (BTC-USD) this week, pushing up implied yields and likely making future funding for BTC purchases more expensive, according to a media report on Friday.

The crypto treasury giant has aggressively tapped debt and equity markets this year to load up on bitcoin (BTC-USD). It has issued billions of dollars in high-yield preferred stock, such as (STRK), (STRF) (STRD) and (STRC), alongside its convertible debt.

But, with bitcoin (BTC-USD) under pressure, a $1.2B, 10% Strategy (MSTR) preferred offering that was priced in June at 85 cents on the dollar has dropped to 66 cents, lifting its yield to about 15%, according to data compiled by Bloomberg, which added that prices on other issues have also fallen.

Beyond raising questions about Strategy's (MSTR) ability to keep raising money for new bitcoin (BTC-USD) buys, the slide in preferreds also clouds its capacity to meet dividend obligations on existing preferred stock. Because those payouts are paid in dollars and euros, the company must continue issuing new securities to generate the cash. The dividends are not payable in BTC.

So far this year, Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR) has sold five preferred stock series, Bloomberg reported, noting bankers usually look to the yields on outstanding preferreds to determine pricing for new offerings. 

Bloomberg pointed out that Strategy (MSTR) is not required to make payments on its existing preferreds and can skip dividend payouts. But such moves risk souring demand for subsequent offerings, and some preferreds grant holders a board seat if the company does not pay dividends.

Strategy (MSTR) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Earlier on Friday, JPMorgan warned that Strategy (MSTR) is at risk of being removed from MSCI's equity indices, a move that could trigger billions of dollars in outflows.

MSTR common stock is on track to close out the week ended Nov. 21 with a 16% drop, coinciding with bitcoin's 12% slump.

data.title: AI companies' use of leverage bears close watching, Fed's Jefferson says
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Philip Jefferson sees important differences between the dot-com boom and bust of the late 1990s and the current surge of valuations in artificial intelligence companies, he said Friday. 

That's not to say there are no concerns about AI's ability to threaten financial stability. For example, in a Fed survey, 30% of contracts considered a turn in the sentiment toward AI as a salient risk to the U.S. financial system and the global economy, up from 9% in a spring survey. "Participants noted that if such an unwinding were to occur, it could tighten financial conditions and restrain economic activity more broadly," Jefferson pointed out. He's not making a judgment on the likelihood of the scenario, but highlights the importance of monitoring such scenarios.

He observed several clear differences between the current AI boom and the dot-com era:

  • Many publicly traded dot-com firms had little or no realized earnings and sometimes only speculative revenue compared with today's AI-focused stocks, which "generally have well-established and growing earnings streams."
  • Price-earnings ratios of AI-related firms have remained well below peak ratios of dot-com companies in the late1990s-early 2000s.
  • "The proliferation of dot-com firms in the late 1990s and early 2000s suggests a less discriminating enthusiasm than what we are observing in today's market," Jefferson said. At its peak in the late 1990s, more than 1,000 firms were publicly listed as dot-come companies. By one measure, only about 50 publicly traded firms are considered to be AI-focused enterprises, he said.

One common factor is that both dot-com and AI firms until recently demonstrated little reliance on debt financing, which may reduce the impact from a shift in sentiment toward AI through credit markets. However, recent reports suggest that AI companies may be increasing their use of debt through public and private credit markets to fund the substantial investments in computing infrastructure and talent acquisition, Jefferson said.

"If that turns out to be the case, leverage in the AI sector could increase—and so could the losses if sentiment toward AI shifts. I will watch this developing trend closely," Jefferson said.

He also emphasized that the developments in AI are taking place against a "backdrop of a financial system that is sound and resilient."

Related ETFs:  iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY); Amplify AI Powered Equity ETF (AIEQ), and REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI).

data.description:
• Insperity (NSP) declares $0.60/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 7.4%
